title: Statistics
text:
Statistics (1)
Statistics (2)




Course Objectives

To train students in basic statistical knowledge and econometric concepts.
To enhance students’ familiarity with data analysis and provide hands-on experience in statistical analysis.

Textbooks

Bluman, A. G. (2012). Elementary Statistics: A Step-by-Step Approach (8th ed.). McGraw-Hill.

Course Structure

Lecture (First hour): Foundational knowledge and concepts introduced each session.
Lab (Second hour): Hands-on in-class exercises using R to apply the concepts learned during the lecture.

Assessment Criteria

Attendance (12%): \(R = \min\{12,\, t\}\), where \(t\) represents the number of sessions attended.
In-Class Exercises (48%): 12 exercises × 4% each, submitted at the end of each session.
Mid-Term Examination (20%) and Final Oral Examination (20%).

Regulations

Attendance

Per Shih Chien University regulations, students who are absent for more than one-third of class sessions will receive a final grade of zero. Make-up attendance will not be permitted.

Examination

Students who do not show up for their scheduled examination will receive a grade of zero for that component.

In-class exercise deadline

The deadline for all in-class exercises is before midnight of the following week. Late submissions will not be accepted.

Academic Integrity

All submitted work must be the student’s own. Copying from other students is strictly prohibited. AI detection tools are used to review submissions; any work found to be copied will receive a grade of zero.

AI Policy

Students may use AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT, GitHub Copilot) as learning aids, but must be able to explain and defend any code or analysis they submit. Uncritical submission of AI-generated work without understanding constitutes academic dishonesty.
Required Software
All software is free and open-source: R at https://cran.r-project.org, RStudio Desktop at https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop, and R packages tidyverse, ggplot2, patchwork (installation instructions on the course R page).

R is the underlying statistical computing environment and must be installed before RStudio.

Navigate to the official CRAN repository: https://cran.r-project.org
Select the installer appropriate for your operating system:

Windows: Click Download R for Windows → base → download and execute the installer
macOS: Click Download R for macOS → select the build matching your architecture (Apple Silicon users should select the arm64 build)
Linux: Follow the distribution-specific installation instructions provided on the page

Run the installer using the default configuration settings.




RStudio is an integrated development environment (IDE) for R. It requires a prior R installation to function.

Navigate to: https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/
Download the free RStudio Desktop release for your operating system
Execute the installer and follow the on-screen instructions
Upon launching RStudio, the application should automatically detect the installed R environment


Important: R and RStudio are distinct pieces of software. RStudio does not bundle R; it serves solely as an interface to an existing R installation.

section: Authoring Documents with R Markdown and Generating PDF Output
text:
Authoring Documents with R Markdown and Generating PDF Output
R Markdown is a document format that integrates prose, code, and computational output within a single reproducible file. The following steps outline the process of creating an R Markdown document and rendering it as a PDF.

Step 1: Install Required Packages
Execute the following commands in the RStudio Console:
install.packages("rmarkdown")
install.packages("knitr")


Step 2: Install a LaTeX Distribution
PDF rendering requires a LaTeX engine. The tinytex package provides a lightweight, self-contained LaTeX distribution suitable for most use cases:
install.packages("tinytex")
tinytex::install_tinytex()
Restart RStudio after installation to ensure the LaTeX binaries are correctly registered.


Step 3: Create a New R Markdown File

From the RStudio menu, select: File → New File → R Markdown…
Specify a title and author in the dialogue box; set the default output format to PDF
Click OK to generate the template file



Step 4: Configure the YAML Header
Every R Markdown document begins with a YAML front matter block delimited by ---. This block controls document metadata and output settings:
---
title: "Assignment Title"
author: "Full Name (Student ID)"
date: "2024-01-01"
output: pdf_document
---


Step 5: Write Document Content
R Markdown uses standard Markdown syntax for formatting prose, supplemented by fenced code chunks for embedding R code:



Syntax
Rendered Output




# Heading 1
First-level heading


## Heading 2
Second-level heading


**bold**
bold


*italic*
italic


Fenced code block with {r}
Executed R code with output



Example code chunk:

summary(mtcars)

Step 6: Render to PDF
Click the Knit button in the RStudio toolbar, or use the keyboard shortcut Ctrl+Shift+K (Windows/Linux) or Cmd+Shift+K (macOS), and select Knit to PDF.

Note on CJK character rendering: If Chinese, Japanese, or Korean characters appear as missing glyphs in the PDF output, specify an alternative LaTeX engine in the YAML header:
output:
  pdf_document:
    latex_engine: xelatex

  - \usepackage{fontspec}
  - \setmainfont{Noto Sans CJK TC}

title: Statistics
text:
Statistics (1) and Statistics (2) are sequential required offerings in the English Taught Program in International Business (ETP) at Shih Chien University, taught by Yu-You Liou. Together, they provide a comprehensive introduction to statistical reasoning and quantitative analysis.
The required textbook is Elementary Statistics: A Step-by-Step Approach (8th ed.) by Allan G. Bluman (McGraw-Hill, 2012). Statistics (1) covers Chapters 1–7 (descriptive statistics, probability, and the foundations of inference), and Statistics (2) covers Chapters 8–12 (hypothesis testing, regression, chi-square tests, and ANOVA).
Each course follows the same structure: a lecture hour for conceptual foundations and a lab hour for hands-on in-class exercises.

title: Prompt
text:
Instructions

Structural & Stylistic Alignment Replicate the exact formatting, structural hierarchy, and stylistic layout of the provided slides (found in the ch2 directory) to ensure visual and pedagogical consistency across all chapters.
Comprehensive Mathematical Examples & R Integration Ensure that all content from the source text, including every example, is thoroughly extracted and incorporated into the output. Each example must be accompanied by a comprehensive, step-by-step solution implemented in R. The final output must explicitly present both the clean R code and its corresponding execution results, mirroring the format of the referenced slides.
Paraphrasing & Academic Integrity To prevent plagiarism, you must completely rewrite and paraphrase all textual descriptions, definitions, explanations, and commentary from the textbook. The narrative flow, tone, and wording must be entirely original, rigorous yet accessible, and tailored specifically for a student audience, while strictly preserving the technical and mathematical accuracy of the original content.
Strategic Use of Callout Blocks Utilize visually distinct callout blocks (e.g., alert/info/note boxes) to segment key information and enhance readability. Specifically, apply callout blocks in the following scenarios:

Definitions & Formulas: Whenever the textbook presents a standard definition box or a key mathematical formula.
Example Prompts: Enclose the initial question/prompt of every example within a callout block to separate it clearly from its solution and R code.

Directory and Storage Architecture Replicate the established file structure utilized in the previous modules. Dynamically generate and store the output within the newly designated directory (e.g., ch3/), precisely mirroring the organization, sub-folders, and naming conventions of the repository.

title: Statistics
section: From Raw Data to Organized Information
text:
From Raw Data to Organized Information
When data are first gathered, they exist in raw form — an unordered, unprocessed list of values. On their own, raw data reveal very little about the underlying patterns or trends in a dataset.
Organizing data into a frequency distribution provides a structured summary that makes patterns visible and analysis manageable.






Frequency Distribution


A frequency distribution is a table that organizes data values (or ranges of values) along with the count of how often each value or range occurs in the dataset.

title: Statistics
text:
Categorical Frequency Distributions
When data consist of qualitative categories (non-numeric labels or groups), a categorical frequency distribution is used. Each category is listed alongside its frequency and relative frequency (percentage).
Steps to construct a categorical frequency distribution:

List each distinct category.
Tally the occurrences of each category.
Record the frequency and compute the percentage: \(\% = \frac{f}{n} \times 100\)

title: Statistics
section: Example 2-1: Blood Types of Army Inductees
text:
Example 2-1: Blood Types of Army Inductees






Example 2-1


A sample of 25 army inductees was tested for blood type. The results are shown below. Organize the data into a categorical frequency distribution.
A  B  B  AB  O
O  O  B  AB  B
B  B  O  A   O
A  O  O  O   AB
AB A  O  B   A

title: Statistics
text:
Example 2-1: Solution

blood <- c("A","B","B","AB","O","O","O","B","AB","B",
           "B","B","O","A","O","A","O","O","O","AB",
           "AB","A","O","B","A")

df <- data.frame(blood) |>
  count(blood) |>
  rename(Class = blood, Frequency = n) |>
  mutate(Percentage = round(Frequency / sum(Frequency) * 100, 1))

knitr::kable(df, align = "c")




Class
Frequency
Percentage




A
5

AB
4
16


B
7
28

title: Statistics
text:
Example 2-1: Interpretation
The frequency distribution reveals that Type O is the most common blood type in this sample, occurring 9 times (36%). Type A appears 5 times (20%), Type B appears 6 times (24%), and Type AB appears 4 times (16%).

ggplot(df, aes(x = reorder(Class, -Frequency), y = Frequency, fill = Class)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Frequency), vjust = -0.3) +
  labs(title = "Blood Types of 25 Army Inductees",
       x = "Blood Type", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Statistics
text:
Grouped Frequency Distributions
When dealing with quantitative data that span a wide range of values, individual values are grouped into classes (intervals). The resulting table is called a grouped frequency distribution.
Key terminology:

Class limits: The smallest (lower) and largest (upper) values that can belong to a class.
Class boundaries: Values halfway between the upper limit of one class and the lower limit of the next, eliminating gaps.
Class width: The difference between consecutive lower class limits (or upper class boundaries).
Class midpoint: \(X_m = \dfrac{\text{lower limit} + \text{upper limit}}{2}\)

title: Statistics
section: Rules for Constructing a Grouped Frequency Distribution
text:
Rules for Constructing a Grouped Frequency Distribution






Six Construction Guidelines



Use between 5 and 20 classes — enough to show pattern without excessive detail.
The class width should be an odd number when possible so midpoints are whole numbers.
Classes must be mutually exclusive — each data value belongs to exactly one class.
Classes must be continuous — no gaps between classes, even if some classes have zero frequency.
Classes must be exhaustive — every data value must fit into some class.
Classes should be equal in width (except possibly the first or last class).

Formula: \(\text{Class width} \approx \dfrac{\text{highest value} - \text{lowest value}}{\text{number of classes}}\)

title: Statistics
text:
Example 2-2: Record High Temperatures






Example 2-2


The record high temperatures (°F) for each of the 50 U.S. states are listed below. Construct a grouped frequency distribution using 7 classes.
112 100 127 120 134 118 105 110 109 112
110 118 117 116 118 122 114 114 105 109
107 112 114 115 118 117 118 122 106 110
116 108 110 121 113 120 119 111 104 111
120 113 120 117 105 110 118 112 114 114

title: Statistics
text:
Example 2-2: Visualization

ggplot(data.frame(temps), aes(x = temps)) +
  geom_histogram(breaks = breaks, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(100, 135, by = 5)) +
  labs(title = "Record High Temperatures for 50 U.S. States",
       x = "Temperature (°F)", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Statistics
text:
Ungrouped Frequency Distributions
When the range of values is small and each distinct value is meaningful on its own, an ungrouped (single-value) frequency distribution lists each individual data value as its own class.
This approach preserves the exact values rather than collapsing them into intervals.

title: Statistics
section: Example 2-3: Miles per Gallon for SUVs
text:
Example 2-3: Miles per Gallon for SUVs






Example 2-3


The fuel economy ratings (mpg) for a sample of 30 sport utility vehicles are listed below. Construct an ungrouped frequency distribution.
12 17 12 14 16 18 16 18 12 16
15 15 16 12 15 15 16 16 12 14
15 12 15 15 12 16 15 16 16 12

title: Statistics
Statistics
text:
Cumulative Frequency Distributions
A cumulative frequency distribution shows the running total of frequencies from the lowest class up through each successive class. It answers the question: “How many observations fall at or below this value?”

title: Statistics
text:
Graphing Grouped Frequency Distributions
Three fundamental graphs are used to visualize data organized in a grouped frequency distribution:

Histogram — bars whose heights represent frequencies (or relative frequencies), with no gaps between bars.
Frequency polygon — a line graph connecting the midpoints of each class at their frequency values; extended to the x-axis at each end.
Ogive (oh-jive) — a cumulative frequency graph; shows the running total of observations up to each class boundary.

title: Statistics
text:
The Histogram






Histogram


A histogram is a bar graph in which the horizontal axis represents the class boundaries and the vertical axis represents the frequencies (or relative frequencies). Adjacent bars share boundaries — there are no gaps.

title: Statistics
section: Example 2-4: Histogram of Record High Temperatures
text:
Example 2-4: Histogram of Record High Temperatures






Example 2-4


Using the frequency distribution from Example 2-2, draw a histogram for the record high temperatures of the 50 U.S. states.

title: Statistics
text:
The Frequency Polygon






Frequency Polygon


A frequency polygon is a line graph drawn by connecting the midpoints of the tops of each histogram bar. The line is extended to the x-axis one class width beyond each end, so the polygon is “closed.”

title: Statistics
text:
Example 2-5: Frequency Polygon






Example 2-5


Draw a frequency polygon for the record high temperatures data from Example 2-2.




midpoints <- seq(102, 132, by = 5)
freqs     <- freq_table$Frequency

poly_df <- data.frame(
  x = c(97, midpoints, 137),
  y = c(0,  freqs,     0)
)

title: Statistics
text:
The Ogive






Ogive


An ogive (cumulative frequency graph) plots the upper class boundaries on the x-axis against the cumulative frequencies on the y-axis. It is useful for determining how many observations fall below a particular value.

title: Statistics
section: Example 2-6: Ogive for Record High Temperatures
text:
Example 2-6: Ogive for Record High Temperatures






Example 2-6


Draw an ogive for the record high temperatures distribution from Example 2-2.




upper_bounds <- c(99.5, 104.5, 109.5, 114.5, 119.5, 124.5, 129.5, 134.5)
cum_freqs    <- c(0, freq_table$Cumulative)

ogive_df <- data.frame(x = upper_bounds, y = cum_freqs)

title: Statistics
text:
Relative Frequency Graphs
All three graph types (histogram, frequency polygon, ogive) can be redrawn using relative frequencies (proportions or percentages) instead of raw counts. The shape of the graph is identical, but the vertical axis reflects the fraction of total observations rather than the count.
Relative frequency graphs facilitate comparisons between datasets of different sizes.

title: Statistics
text:
Example 2-7: Solution
Prepare data

run_df <- data.frame(
  lower = c(5.5, 10.5, 15.5, 20.5, 25.5, 30.5, 35.5),
  upper = c(10.5,15.5, 20.5, 25.5, 30.5, 35.5, 40.5),
  freq  = c(1, 2, 3, 5, 4, 3, 2)
) |>
  mutate(
    mid    = (lower + upper) / 2,
    rel_f  = freq / sum(freq),
    cum_rf = cumsum(rel_f)
  )

title: Statistics
text:
Distribution Shapes
The overall shape of a distribution describes how data values are spread across the range. Recognizing shape helps in choosing appropriate statistical methods.

title: Statistics
text:
Visualizing Distribution Shapes

set.seed(42)

make_hist <- function(data, title, fill_col) {
  ggplot(data.frame(x = data), aes(x)) +
    geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = fill_col, color = "white", linewidth = 0.3) +
    labs(title = title, x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 9) +
    theme(axis.text = element_blank())
}

p_bell    <- make_hist(rnorm(500, 50, 10),     "Bell-shaped",    "#4a90d9")
p_uni     <- make_hist(runif(500, 20, 80),     "Uniform",        "#f0ad4e")
p_right   <- make_hist(rexp(500, 0.05),        "Right-skewed",   "#d9534f")
p_left    <- make_hist(80 - rexp(500, 0.05),   "Left-skewed",    "#5cb85c")
p_bimod   <- make_hist(c(rnorm(250,30,5), rnorm(250,70,5)), "Bimodal", "#9b59b6")
p_u       <- make_hist(c(rnorm(200,20,5), rnorm(200,80,5)), "U-shaped", "#1abc9c")

title: Statistics
text:
Beyond the Histogram
In addition to histograms, frequency polygons, and ogives, several other graphical tools are widely used in statistics and everyday data communication:

Bar graphs — for comparing frequencies of categorical data
Pareto charts — bar graphs with bars sorted from highest to lowest frequency
Time series graphs — for tracking a variable’s change over time
Pie graphs — for showing each category’s share of the whole
Stem and leaf plots — for displaying the actual data values in a graphical form

title: Statistics
text:
Bar Graphs






Bar Graph


A bar graph represents categorical (qualitative) data using horizontal or vertical bars. The length or height of each bar corresponds to the frequency or value for that category. Unlike a histogram, gaps between bars are acceptable.

title: Statistics
text:
Pareto Charts






Pareto Chart


A Pareto chart is a specialized bar graph in which the categories are arranged in descending order of frequency. It is particularly useful for identifying which categories account for the largest share of the total — a key concept in quality control and decision-making.



The name honors Vilfredo Pareto, who observed that a small number of causes often account for the majority of effects (the “80/20 rule”).

Time Series Graphs






Time Series Graph


A time series graph plots a quantitative variable on the vertical axis against time on the horizontal axis. The data points are connected by line segments to emphasize trend or pattern over time.

  year  = 2003:2008,

       x = "Year", y = "Number of Homicides") +
  theme_minimal()

title: Statistics
text:
Pie Graphs






Pie Graph


A pie graph (pie chart) is a circle divided into sectors, where each sector represents a category. The size of each sector is proportional to that category’s share of the total.
Formula for sector angle: \[\text{Degrees} = \frac{f}{n} \times 360°\]
where \(f\) is the category frequency and \(n\) is the total number of observations.

title: Statistics
text:
Example 2-11: Solution

  item = c("Potato chips","Tortilla chips","Pretzels","Popcorn","Snack nuts"),
  freq = c(11.2, 8.2, 4.3, 3.8, 2.5)

title: Statistics
text:
Misleading Graphs






Caution: Graphs Can Mislead


Graphs are powerful communication tools, but they can distort perception. Common misleading techniques include:

Truncating the y-axis — starting the vertical axis at a value other than zero exaggerates differences between bars or points.
Using two-dimensional images for one-dimensional changes — the eye compares areas rather than heights, making small differences appear dramatic.
Omitting axis labels or units — without a labeled y-axis, there is no way to gauge the actual magnitude of differences.

Always examine the scale and labels carefully before drawing conclusions from a graph.

title: Statistics
text:
Stem and Leaf Plots






Stem and Leaf Plot


A stem and leaf plot (stemplot) organizes data by splitting each value into a stem (the leading digit(s)) and a leaf (the trailing digit). This technique preserves the actual data values while displaying them in a graphical form that reveals the shape of the distribution.

Each row represents one stem (class).
Leaves within each row are arranged in ascending order.
The plot functions like a histogram turned on its side.

title: Statistics
section: Example 2-15: Back-to-Back Stem and Leaf Plot
text:
Example 2-15: Back-to-Back Stem and Leaf Plot






Back-to-Back Stem and Leaf Plot


When comparing two related groups, a back-to-back stem and leaf plot uses a shared column of stems in the center, with one group’s leaves fanning left and the other’s fanning right. This makes it easy to compare the shapes, centers, and spreads of both distributions simultaneously.

text:
Example 2-15: Tall Buildings in Atlanta vs. Philadelphia






Example 2-15


The number of stories in samples of tall buildings from Atlanta and Philadelphia are shown. Construct a back-to-back stem and leaf plot and compare the two distributions.
Atlanta: 55, 70, 44, 36, 40, 63, 40, 44, 34, 38, 60, 47, 52, 32, 32, 50, 53, 32, 28, 31, 52, 32, 34, 32, 50, 26, 29
Philadelphia: 61, 40, 38, 32, 30, 58, 40, 40, 25, 30, 54, 40, 36, 30, 30, 53, 39, 36, 34, 33, 50, 38, 36, 39, 32

title: Statistics
text:
Example 2-15: Interpretation
Examining the back-to-back plot reveals several key differences:

Both distributions peak in the 30-story range, meaning this is the most common height class in both cities.
Atlanta shows considerably more variability — its buildings range from the upper 20s all the way to 70 stories.
Philadelphia buildings are more tightly concentrated in the 30–50 story range.
Atlanta has more buildings of 40 or more stories, indicating a slightly taller skyline in this sample.

Graph Type

title: Statistics
text:
Key Formulas — Chapter 2






Important Formulas


\[\text{Class width} \approx \frac{\text{highest} - \text{lowest}}{\text{number of classes}}\]
\[X_m = \frac{\text{lower limit} + \text{upper limit}}{2} \quad \text{(class midpoint)}\]
\[\% = \frac{f}{n} \times 100 \quad \text{(percentage for each class)}\]
\[\text{Degrees} = \frac{f}{n} \times 360° \quad \text{(pie chart sector)}\]

title: Statistics
text:
Chapter 2: Key Vocabulary

Raw data — unorganized observations collected before any processing
Frequency distribution — a table pairing data values/classes with their counts
Class limits / boundaries / midpoint / width — structural components of a grouped distribution
Cumulative frequency — running total of frequencies up to and including a class
Histogram / frequency polygon / ogive — the three standard distribution graphs
Bar graph / Pareto chart / time series / pie graph — graphs for qualitative or time-based data
Stem and leaf plot — a data display that retains individual values in graphical form
Distribution shape — the overall pattern of a frequency distribution (symmetric, skewed, bimodal, etc.)

title: Statistics
section: What Is a Measure of Central Tendency?
text:
What Is a Measure of Central Tendency?
A measure of central tendency is a single numerical value that summarizes the center or “typical” value of a dataset. Rather than examining every individual observation, a central tendency statistic provides a concise snapshot of where data tend to cluster.
The four most widely used measures are:

Mean — arithmetic average of all values
Median — middle value when data are ordered
Mode — most frequently occurring value(s)
Midrange — midpoint between the highest and lowest values

title: Statistics
text:
The Mean
The mean is computed by summing all data values and dividing by the number of observations. It is the most commonly reported measure of center.






Formulas for the Mean


Sample mean: \[\bar{X} = \frac{\Sigma X}{n}\]
Population mean: \[\mu = \frac{\Sigma X}{N}\]
where \(\Sigma X\) is the sum of all data values and \(n\) (or \(N\)) is the count of values.









Rounding Rule


Round the mean to one more decimal place than appears in the original data.

days_off <- c(13, 15, 26, 36, 37, 42)

Sum of values: 169

title: Statistics
text:
Mean for Grouped Data
When raw data are unavailable and only a frequency distribution is given, use the midpoint of each class to approximate the mean.






Formula — Mean for Grouped Data


\[\bar{X} = \frac{\Sigma f \cdot X_m}{n}\]
where \(f\) = class frequency, \(X_m\) = class midpoint, \(n = \Sigma f\).

title: Statistics
text:
The Median
The median is the value that sits at the exact center of an ordered dataset, dividing it into two equal halves. It is resistant to the influence of extreme values.






Finding the Median



Arrange all data values from lowest to highest.
If \(n\) is odd: the median is the middle value — position \(\frac{n+1}{2}\).
If \(n\) is even: the median is the average of the two middle values.

title: Statistics
text:
Example 3-4: Hotel Rooms






Example 3-4


The number of rooms in the seven hotels in a downtown district are listed below. Find the median.
Rooms: 713, 300, 618, 595, 311, 401, 292

title: Statistics
text:
Example 3-4: Solution

rooms <- c(713, 300, 618, 595, 311, 401, 292)
sorted <- sort(rooms)
cat("Sorted:", sorted, "\n")

Sorted: 292 300 311 401 595 618 713 

cat("Median position:", (length(rooms)+1)/2, "\n")

Median position: 4 

cat("Median:", median(rooms), "\n")

Median: 401 


With 7 values (odd), the median is the 4th value when sorted: 401 rooms.

title: Statistics
text:
Example 3-5: National Park Costs (Even n)






Example 3-5


The entrance fees (in dollars) for a random sample of national parks are: 25, 10, 8, 15, 5, 12, 14, 3. Find the median.




fees <- c(25, 10, 8, 15, 5, 12, 14, 3)
sorted_fees <- sort(fees)
cat("Sorted:", sorted_fees, "\n")

Sorted: 3 5 8 10 12 14 15 25 

cat("n =", length(fees), "(even) → average of positions",
    length(fees)/2, "and", length(fees)/2 + 1, "\n")

n = 8 (even) → average of positions 4 and 5 

cat("Median:", median(fees), "\n")

Median: 11 


With 8 values (even), the median is the average of the 4th and 5th sorted values: \(\frac{10+12}{2}\) = $11.

title: Statistics
text:
The Mode
The mode is the value that appears most frequently in a dataset. Unlike the mean and median, the mode can be used with both quantitative and qualitative data.






Key Properties of the Mode



A dataset may have no mode (all values appear once), one mode (unimodal), two modes (bimodal), or more (multimodal).
For grouped data, the modal class is the class with the highest frequency.
Mode is the only appropriate measure of center for nominal (categorical) data.

title: Statistics
text:
Example 3-9: NFL Signing Bonuses






Example 3-9


The signing bonuses (in millions of dollars) for a sample of NFL players are listed. Find the mode.
Values: 18, 14, 10, 10, 10, 7, 7, 3, 2, 2, 2, 8




bonuses <- c(18, 14, 10, 10, 10, 7, 7, 3, 2, 2, 2, 8)

mode_tbl <- sort(table(bonuses), decreasing = TRUE)
print(mode_tbl)

bonuses
 2 10  7  3  8 14 18 
 3  3  2  1  1  1  1 

cat("Mode: $", names(mode_tbl)[1], "million\n")

Mode: $ 2 million


Both 2 and 10 appear 3 times → dataset is bimodal: $2M and $10M.

title: Statistics
text:

The Midrange
The midrange is the arithmetic mean of the maximum and minimum data values. It is easy to calculate but highly sensitive to extreme values.






Formula — Midrange

The Weighted Mean
When different values carry different levels of importance (weight), use the weighted mean to compute an accurate average.






Formula — Weighted Mean


\[\bar{X}_w = \frac{\Sigma w \cdot X}{\Sigma w}\]
where \(w\) = weight and \(X\) = value.

section: Choosing the Right Measure of Center
text:
Choosing the Right Measure of Center






Which Measure to Use?





Situation
Best Measure




Most typical value desired
Mode


Distribution is open-ended (no max/min)
Median


Extreme values are present
Median


Data are categorical (nominal)
Mode


Further computations are needed
Mean


Data must be split into two equal groups
Median



The mean is generally preferred when data are approximately symmetric with no extreme outliers, since it uses every data value in its computation.

title: Statistics
text:
Why Variation Matters
Two datasets can have identical means yet differ dramatically in their spread. Measures of variation quantify how spread out or clustered data values are around the center.






Three Key Measures of Variation





Measure
Symbol(s)
Description




Range
\(R\)
Distance between highest and lowest


Variance
\(\sigma^2\), \(s^2\)
Average squared distance from mean


Standard Deviation
\(\sigma\), \(s\)
Square root of variance

title: Statistics
section: Example 3-18: Why the Mean Alone Is Insufficient
text:
Example 3-18: Why the Mean Alone Is Insufficient






Example 3-18


A testing laboratory evaluates two brands of outdoor paint to see how long (in months) each lasts before fading. Six cans of each brand were tested.
Brand A: 10, 60, 50, 30, 40, 20 — Mean = 35 months Brand B: 35, 45, 30, 35, 40, 25 — Mean = 35 months




A <- c(10, 60, 50, 30, 40, 20)
B <- c(35, 45, 30, 35, 40, 25)
cat("Brand A mean:", mean(A), "| Brand B mean:", mean(B), "\n")

Brand A mean: 35 | Brand B mean: 35

title: Statistics
text:
The Range
The range is the simplest measure of spread — the difference between the largest and smallest values.






Formula — Range


\[R = \text{highest value} - \text{lowest value}\]









Limitation of the Range


A single extreme value can drastically inflate the range, making it a misleading indicator of typical variation.

title: Statistics
text:
Population Variance and Standard Deviation
The variance measures the average squared deviation of each value from the mean. The standard deviation is its square root, expressed in the original units.






Population Formulas


\[\sigma^2 = \frac{\Sigma(X - \mu)^2}{N} \qquad \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{\Sigma(X - \mu)^2}{N}}\]
Step-by-step procedure:

Find the population mean \(\mu\).
Subtract \(\mu\) from each value: \((X - \mu)\).
Square each difference: \((X - \mu)^2\).
Sum all squared differences.
Divide by \(N\) to get variance; take the square root for \(\sigma\).

title: Statistics
text:
Sample Variance and Standard Deviation
When data come from a sample (not the entire population), divide by \(n - 1\) rather than \(n\) to obtain an unbiased estimate of the population variance.






Sample Formulas


\[s^2 = \frac{\Sigma(X - \bar{X})^2}{n-1} \qquad s = \sqrt{\frac{\Sigma(X - \bar{X})^2}{n-1}}\]
Shortcut (computational) formulas:
\[s^2 = \frac{n(\Sigma X^2) - (\Sigma X)^2}{n(n-1)} \qquad s = \sqrt{\frac{n(\Sigma X^2) - (\Sigma X)^2}{n(n-1)}}\]









Why \(n-1\)?


Dividing by \(n-1\) corrects the tendency of sample variance to underestimate population variance. The value \(n-1\) is called the degrees of freedom.

title: Statistics
text:
Coefficient of Variation
The coefficient of variation (CVar) expresses the standard deviation as a percentage of the mean, enabling comparison of variability across datasets with different units or different scales.






Formula — Coefficient of Variation


\[\text{CVar} = \frac{s}{\bar{X}} \times 100\% \quad \text{(samples)} \qquad \text{CVar} = \frac{\sigma}{\mu} \times 100\% \quad \text{(populations)}\]

text:

The mean number of car sales per 3-month period is 87 with a standard deviation of 5. The mean commission amount is $5,225 with a standard deviation of $773. Which is more variable?

cat("CVar (sales):", round(cvar_sales, 1), "%\n")

CVar (sales): 5.7 %

cat("CVar (commissions):", round(cvar_comm, 1), "%\n")

CVar (commissions): 14.8 %

cat("\nConclusion: Commissions are more variable (larger CVar).\n")


Conclusion: Commissions are more variable (larger CVar).

text:

For women’s fitness magazines: mean pages = 132, variance = 23; mean ads = 182, variance = 62. Which is more variable?

cat("CVar (pages):", round(cvar_pages, 1), "%\n")

CVar (pages): 3.6 %

cat("CVar (ads):", round(cvar_ads, 1), "%\n")

CVar (ads): 4.3 %

cat("\nConclusion: Advertisements are more variable.\n")

title: Statistics
text:
Range Rule of Thumb
The range rule of thumb provides a quick approximation of the standard deviation using only the range.






Range Rule of Thumb


\[s \approx \frac{\text{range}}{4}\]
Conversely, most data values fall within the interval: \[[\bar{X} - 2s, \; \bar{X} + 2s]\]



This approximation works best for unimodal, roughly symmetric distributions.

title: Statistics
text:
Chebyshev’s Theorem
Chebyshev’s theorem provides a guaranteed lower bound on the proportion of data values that fall within \(k\) standard deviations of the mean — regardless of the distribution’s shape.






Chebyshev’s Theorem


The proportion of values from any data set that will fall within \(k\) standard deviations of the mean is at least:
\[1 - \frac{1}{k^2}, \quad k > 1\]



\(k\)
Minimum % within \(k\) SDs




2
At least 75%


3
At least 88.89%


2.5
At least 84%

title: Statistics
text:
Example 3-27: Home Prices (Chebyshev)






Example 3-27


The mean price of homes in a neighborhood is $50,000 and the standard deviation is $10,000. Find the price range in which at least 75% of homes will sell.




mu  <- 50000
sd  <- 10000
k   <- 2   # gives at least 75%

lower <- mu - k * sd
upper <- mu + k * sd
pct   <- (1 - 1/k^2) * 100

cat("k =", k, "→ at least", pct, "% of values\n")

k = 2 → at least 75 % of values

cat("Price range: $", lower, "to $", upper, "\n")

Price range: $ 30000 to $ 70000 


At least 75% of homes will sell between $30,000 and $70,000.

title: Statistics
text:
The Empirical (Normal) Rule
When a distribution is bell-shaped (normal), tighter guarantees apply:






The Empirical Rule


For bell-shaped distributions:



Interval
Approximate % of data




\(\bar{X} \pm 1s\)
≈ 68%


\(\bar{X} \pm 2s\)
≈ 95%


\(\bar{X} \pm 3s\)
≈ 99.7%

title: Statistics
section: Locating a Value Within a Distribution
text:
Locating a Value Within a Distribution
Measures of position describe where a particular value stands in relation to the rest of the dataset. They answer the question: How does this value compare to others?
Key position measures include:

Standard score (z-score) — distance from mean in standard deviation units
Percentiles — divide data into 100 equal groups
Deciles — divide data into 10 equal groups
Quartiles — divide data into 4 equal groups

title: Statistics
text:
Standard Scores (z-Scores)
A z-score (standard score) converts a raw value into the number of standard deviations it lies above or below the mean, enabling fair comparison across different distributions.






z-Score Formulas


\[z = \frac{X - \bar{X}}{s} \quad \text{(sample)} \qquad z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} \quad \text{(population)}\]

\(z > 0\): value is above the mean
\(z = 0\): value equals the mean
\(z < 0\): value is below the mean

title: Statistics
text:
Example 3-29: Comparing Test Performance






Example 3-29


A student scored 65 on a calculus exam (\(\bar{X}=50\), \(s=10\)) and 30 on a history exam (\(\bar{X}=25\), \(s=5\)). On which test did she perform relatively better?




z_calc <- (65 - 50) / 10
z_hist <- (30 - 25) / 5

cat("Calculus z-score:", z_calc, "\n")

Calculus z-score: 1.5 

cat("History z-score:", z_hist, "\n")

History z-score: 1 

cat("\nBoth z-scores are equal (1.5) → same relative performance.\n")


Both z-scores are equal (1.5) → same relative performance.


Both scores are 1.5 standard deviations above their respective means — equal relative positions.

title: Statistics
section: Example 3-30: Which Score Is Relatively Higher?
text:
Example 3-30: Which Score Is Relatively Higher?






Example 3-30





Test
Score (X)

SD (\(s\))




A
38

5


B

Find each z-score and identify the relatively higher score.




z_A <- (38 - 40) / 5
z_B <- (94 - 100) / 10

cat("Test A z-score:", z_A, "\n")

Test A z-score: -0.4 

cat("Test B z-score:", z_B, "\n")

Test B z-score: -0.6 

cat("\nConclusion: Test A score is relatively higher (z = -0.4 > -0.6)\n")


Conclusion: Test A score is relatively higher (z = -0.4 > -0.6)


The Test A score has a higher relative position (z = −0.4 vs z = −0.6).

title: Statistics
text:
Percentiles
Percentiles locate a data value’s position in hundredths. A value at the \(p\)th percentile exceeds \(p\)% of all values in the distribution.






Percentile Formula


\[\text{Percentile rank of } X = \frac{(\text{number of values below } X) + 0.5}{\text{total number of values}} \times 100\]
To find the value corresponding to a given percentile \(p\):
\[c = \frac{n \cdot p}{100}\]

If \(c\) is not a whole number: round up and use that position.
If \(c\) is a whole number: average the \(c\)th and \((c+1)\)th values.

title: Statistics
text:
Quartiles and Deciles
Quartiles divide the dataset into four equal groups (\(Q_1\), \(Q_2\), \(Q_3\)), while deciles divide it into ten groups (\(D_1, \ldots, D_9\)).






Key Relationships





Position Measure
Percentile Equivalent




\(Q_1\) (1st quartile)
\(P_{25}\)


\(Q_2\) (2nd quartile = median)
\(P_{50}\)


\(Q_3\) (3rd quartile)
\(P_{75}\)


\(D_1\) (1st decile)
\(P_{10}\)


\(D_5\) (5th decile)
\(P_{50}\)



Interquartile Range (IQR): \(\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1\)
The IQR measures the spread of the middle 50% of the data.

title: Statistics
text:
Finding Quartiles — Procedure






Step-by-Step Procedure for Q1, Q2, Q3



Arrange data from lowest to highest.
Find the median (\(Q_2\)) of the entire dataset.
Find the median of the lower half (below \(Q_2\)) → this is \(Q_1\).
Find the median of the upper half (above \(Q_2\)) → this is \(Q_3\).

title: Statistics
text:
Example 3-36: Finding Quartiles






Example 3-36


Find \(Q_1\), \(Q_2\), and \(Q_3\) for the data set: 15, 13, 6, 5, 12, 50, 22, 18




x <- c(15, 13, 6, 5, 12, 50, 22, 18)
xs <- sort(x)
cat("Sorted data:", xs, "\n\n")

title: Statistics
text:
Outliers
An outlier is an extremely high or low data value that stands apart from the rest of the distribution. Outliers can distort the mean and standard deviation significantly.






Procedure for Identifying Outliers



Arrange data and find \(Q_1\) and \(Q_3\).
Compute \(\text{IQR} = Q_3 - Q_1\).
Multiply: \(1.5 \times \text{IQR}\).
Compute fences: lower fence \(= Q_1 - 1.5(\text{IQR})\); upper fence \(= Q_3 + 1.5(\text{IQR})\).
Any value outside these fences is a suspected outlier.

title: Statistics
text:
Example 3-37: Checking for Outliers






Example 3-37


Check the following dataset for outliers: 5, 6, 12, 13, 15, 18, 22, 50
(This is the sorted version of Example 3-36’s data.)




x <- c(5, 6, 12, 13, 15, 18, 22, 50)

Q1 <- 9; Q3 <- 20  # from Example 3-36
IQR_val <- Q3 - Q1

lower_fence <- Q1 - 1.5 * IQR_val
upper_fence <- Q3 + 1.5 * IQR_val

title: Statistics
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)
Exploratory data analysis (EDA), introduced by John Tukey, uses graphical and resistant statistics to uncover patterns, distribution shape, and potential outliers without assuming a particular model.






Traditional vs. EDA Approaches





Traditional
Exploratory Data Analysis




Frequency distribution
Stem-and-leaf plot


Histogram
Boxplot


Mean
Median


Standard deviation
Interquartile range (IQR)



EDA statistics (median, IQR) are resistant — less influenced by extreme values — while mean and standard deviation are nonresistant.

title: Statistics
text:
The Five-Number Summary
A five-number summary captures the full spread and shape of a distribution using five key values:






Five-Number Summary



Minimum — lowest data value
\(Q_1\) — first quartile (25th percentile)
Median (\(Q_2\)) — middle value (50th percentile)
\(Q_3\) — third quartile (75th percentile)
Maximum — highest data value




These five values form the basis of the boxplot (box-and-whisker plot).

title: Statistics
text:
Constructing a Boxplot






Steps for Building a Boxplot



Find the five-number summary.
Draw a horizontal axis scaled to include the min and max.
Draw a box from \(Q_1\) to \(Q_3\) with a vertical line at the median.
Extend whiskers from the box to the minimum and maximum values.




Reading the Shape from a Boxplot:

Median near center of box → approximately symmetric
Median left of center → positively skewed (right-skewed)
Median right of center → negatively skewed (left-skewed)
Longer right whisker → positively skewed
Longer left whisker → negatively skewed

title: Statistics
text:
Summary: Chapter 3 at a Glance






Section 3-1: Measures of Central Tendency


Mean \(\bar{X} = \frac{\Sigma X}{n}\) — uses all values; sensitive to outliers
Median — middle value; resistant to outliers
Mode — most frequent; can be used with categorical data
Midrange \(= \frac{\min + \max}{2}\) — easily computed; sensitive to extremes
Weighted Mean \(= \frac{\Sigma wX}{\Sigma w}\) — accounts for different weights









Section 3-2: Measures of Variation


Range \(R = \max - \min\)
Variance \(s^2 = \frac{n(\Sigma X^2)-(\Sigma X)^2}{n(n-1)}\)
Standard Deviation \(s = \sqrt{s^2}\)
CVar \(= \frac{s}{\bar{X}} \times 100\%\) — compares variability across datasets
Chebyshev’s Theorem — at least \(1-\frac{1}{k^2}\) within \(k\) SDs (any distribution)
Empirical Rule — 68/95/99.7% within 1/2/3 SDs (bell-shaped only)

title: Statistics
text:
Summary: Chapter 3 at a Glance (cont.)






Section 3-3: Measures of Position


z-score \(= \frac{X - \bar{X}}{s}\) — standard deviations from the mean
Percentile rank \(= \frac{(\text{# below }X)+0.5}{n} \times 100\)
Quartiles: \(Q_1 = P_{25}\), \(Q_2 = P_{50}\), \(Q_3 = P_{75}\)
IQR \(= Q_3 - Q_1\) — spread of the middle 50%
Outlier fences: \(Q_1 - 1.5(\text{IQR})\) and \(Q_3 + 1.5(\text{IQR})\)









Section 3-4: Exploratory Data Analysis


Five-number summary: Min, \(Q_1\), Median, \(Q_3\), Max
Boxplot: visual display of five-number summary revealing center, spread, and skew
EDA statistics (median, IQR) are resistant to outliers
Traditional statistics (mean, SD) are nonresistant

Chapter Overview
Chapter 4: Probability and Counting Rules
Probability is the foundation of statistical inference. This chapter covers:

Section 4-1: Sample Spaces and Probability

title: Statistics
text:
Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

Determine sample spaces and compute classical probability
Compute empirical probability using frequency distributions
Find the probability of compound events using the addition rules
Find the probability of compound events using the multiplication rules
Compute conditional probability
Find the total number of outcomes using the fundamental counting rule
Find the number of ways using the permutation rule
Find the number of ways using the combination rule

title: Statistics

Key Vocabulary







Probability Experiment


A probability experiment is a process that leads to well-defined results called outcomes.











Sample Space and Events



Sample space (S): the set of all possible outcomes of a probability experiment
Outcome: a single result from one trial
Event (E): one or more outcomes — a subset of the sample space
Simple event: an event with exactly one outcome
Compound event: an event with more than one outcome

title: Statistics
text:
Classical Probability







Classical Probability


When all outcomes in the sample space are equally likely:
\[P(E) = \frac{n(E)}{n(S)} = \frac{\text{number of outcomes in event } E}{\text{total outcomes in sample space}}\]











Probability Rules



\(0 \leq P(E) \leq 1\) for any event \(E\)
\(P(S) = 1\) (sum of all outcomes = 1)
\(P(\emptyset) = 0\) (impossible event)
\(\sum P(E_i) = 1\) for all simple events

Complement Rule


The complement of event \(E\), denoted \(\bar{E}\), consists of all outcomes not in \(E\).
\[P(\bar{E}) = 1 - P(E)\]

Types of Probability







Three Types of Probability










Type
Definition
Example




Classical
Equal outcomes; based on theory
Rolling a fair die


Empirical
Based on observation/experiment
Frequency survey data


Subjective

Law of Large Numbers


As the number of trials of an experiment increases, the empirical probability approaches the theoretical (classical) probability.

title: Statistics
text:
Mutually Exclusive Events







Mutually Exclusive Events


Two events \(A\) and \(B\) are mutually exclusive if they cannot occur at the same time — they share no outcomes in common.











Addition Rule 1 — Mutually Exclusive Events


When two events \(A\) and \(B\) are mutually exclusive: \[P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B)\]











Addition Rule 2 — Non-Mutually Exclusive Events


When \(A\) and \(B\) are not mutually exclusive: \[P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \text{ and } B)\]

title: Statistics
section: Example 4-15: Rolling a Die — Mutual Exclusivity
text:
Example 4-15: Rolling a Die — Mutual Exclusivity







Example 4-15 — Rolling a Die


Determine which pairs of events are mutually exclusive when a single die is rolled:


odd number and even number;


getting a 3 and getting an odd number;


odd number and number less than 4;


number greater than 4 and number less than 4.








(a) Odd: 1 3 5 | Even: 2 4 6 | Overlap:  → MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE


(b) {3} vs Odd: 1 3 5 | Overlap: 3 → NOT mutually exclusive


(c) Odd: 1 3 5 vs <4: 1 2 3 | Overlap: 1 3 → NOT mutually exclusive


(d) >4: 5 6 vs <4: 1 2 3 | Overlap:  → MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE

title: Statistics
section: Example 4-16: Drawing a Card — Mutual Exclusivity
text:
Example 4-16: Drawing a Card — Mutual Exclusivity







Example 4-16 — Drawing a Card


Determine which events are mutually exclusive when drawing one card:


7 and jack;


club and king;


face card and ace;


face card and spade.








(a) Getting a 7 AND a jack: cannot both be on one card → MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE


(b) Getting a club AND a king: king of clubs exists → NOT mutually exclusive


(c) Face card AND ace: aces are not face cards → MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE


(d) Face card AND spade: jack/queen/king of spades exist → NOT mutually exclusive

text:
Independent and Dependent Events







Independent Events


Two events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if the occurrence of \(A\) does not affect the probability of \(B\) occurring.











Multiplication Rule 1 — Independent Events


\[P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\]
For multiple independent events: \(P(A_1 \text{ and } A_2 \cdots \text{ and } A_k) = P(A_1) \cdot P(A_2) \cdots P(A_k)\)

title: Statistics
text:
Dependent Events







Dependent Events


When the outcome or probability of the first event affects the probability of the second event, the events are dependent.











Multiplication Rule 2 — Dependent Events


\[P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \cdot P(B|A)\]
where \(P(B|A)\) is the conditional probability of \(B\) given \(A\) has occurred.

P(H and A) = P(H) × P(A|H) = 0.53 × 0.27 = 0.1431

3. P(C,S,H)    = 13/52 × 13/51 × 13/50 = 0.016569 = 169/10200

title: Statistics
text:
Conditional Probability







Conditional Probability Formula


The conditional probability of event \(B\) given event \(A\) has occurred:
\[P(B|A) = \frac{P(A \text{ and } B)}{P(A)}\]

title: Statistics
text:
Three Counting Rules







Three Counting Rules


To determine the total number of possible outcomes:

Fundamental Counting Rule: multiply the number of choices at each step
Permutation Rule: arrangements where order matters
Combination Rule: selections where order does not matter












Fundamental Counting Rule


In a sequence of \(n\) events where the first has \(k_1\) possibilities, the second \(k_2\), etc.: \[\text{Total outcomes} = k_1 \cdot k_2 \cdot k_3 \cdots k_n\]

title: Statistics
text:
Factorial Notation and Permutations







Factorial Notation


\[n! = n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2) \cdots 1 \qquad \text{and} \qquad 0! = 1\]











Permutation Rule


An arrangement of \(n\) objects taking \(r\) at a time (order matters): \[_nP_r = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!}\]

title: Statistics
text:
Example 4-42: Business Location







Example 4-42 — Business Location


A business owner ranks all 5 locations. How many different rankings are possible?






5! = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120 different rankings

title: Statistics
text:
Example 4-43 & 4-44: Permutations







Example 4-43 & 4-44: Permutations



How many ways to rank the top 3 of 5 locations?
The advertising director for a television show has 7 ads to use on the program. If she selects 1 of them for the opening of the show, 1 for the middle of the show, and 1 for the ending of the show, how many possible ways can this be accomplished?








Example 4-43: ₅P₃ = 5!/(5-3)! = 5!/2! = 60 



Example 4-44: ₇P₃ = 7!/(7-3)! = 210 ways to place 3 of 7 ads



# Example 4-43
n <- 5; r <- 3
factorial(n) / factorial(n-r)

factorial(n2) / factorial(n2-r2)

text:
Example 4-45: School Musical Plays







Example 4-45 — School Musical Plays


A director selects 2 of 9 plays — one for fall, one for spring. How many arrangements are possible?






₉P₂ = 9!/(9-2)! = 9!/7! = 72 different arrangements



n <- 9; r <- 2
factorial(n) / factorial(n-r)

title: Statistics
text:
Combinations







Combination Rule


A selection of \(r\) objects from \(n\) objects where order does not matter: \[_nC_r = \frac{n!}{(n-r)!\, r!}\]




Key distinction: In a permutation, AB ≠ BA. In a combination, AB = BA.

title: Statistics
text:

Example 4-46 — Letters A, B, C, D


List permutations and combinations of selecting 2 letters from {A, B, C, D}.

[1] 12

factorial(n) / factorial(n-r) / factorial(r)

[1] 6

text:

How many combinations of 4 objects taken 2 at a time?
A newspaper editor has received 8 books to review. He decides that he can use 3 reviews in his newspaper. How many different ways can these 3 reviews be selected?







Example 4-47:


₄C₂ = 4!/(4-2)!2! = 4!/(2!2!) = 6 


Example 4-48: A newspaper editor has 8 books; selects 3 to review.


₈C₃ = 8!/(8-3)!3! = 8!/(5!3!) = 56

title: Statistics

Combining Counting and Probability







Strategy


Use counting rules to determine the number of outcomes in the event and in the sample space, then apply the classical probability formula: \[P(E) = \frac{n(E)}{n(S)}\]

title: Statistics
text:
Example 4-50: Four Aces







Example 4-50 — Four Aces


Find the probability of getting 4 aces when 5 cards are drawn from an ordinary deck.






Total 5-card hands: C(52,5) = 2598960 


Ways to get 4 aces: C(4,4) × C(48,1) = 48 


P(4 aces) = 48 / 2598960 = 1.8e-05 = 1/54145



choose(4, 4)*choose(48, 1)/choose(52, 5)

[1] 1.846893e-05

title: Statistics
text:
Example 4-51: Defective Transistors







Example 4-51 — Defective Transistors


A box has 24 transistors, 4 defective. If 4 are sold at random, find:

P(exactly 2 defective),
P(none defective),
P(all defective),
P(at least 1 defective).







Total ways to select 4 from 24: C(24,4) = 10626 


(a) P(exactly 2 defective) = C(4,2)×C(20,2)/C(24,4) = 1140 / 10626 = 0.1073 


(b) P(none defective)      = C(20,4)/C(24,4) = 4845 / 10626 = 0.456 


(c) P(all defective)       = 1 / 10626 = 9.411e-05 


(d) P(at least 1 defective)= 1 - 0.456 = 0.544

title: Statistics
text:
Example 4-52: Magazines







Example 4-52 — Magazines


A store has 6 TV Graphic and 8 Newstime magazines. Two customers each buy one magazine. Find P(one of each type).






Ways to select 1 of each: C(6,1) × C(8,1) = 48 


Total ways to select 2 from 14: C(14,2) = 91 


P(one of each) = 48 / 91 = 0.5275 = 48/91



choose(6,1) * choose(8,1) / choose(14,2)

[1] 0.5274725

title: Statistics
text:
Example 4-53: Combination Lock







Example 4-53 — Combination Lock


A combination lock uses the 26 letters of the alphabet. Find the probability that a 3-letter combination is ABC in that exact order (repetitions allowed).






Total 3-letter combinations (with repetition): 26³ = 17576 


P(ABC in order) = 1/ 17576 = 5.69e-05 



1 / 26^3

[1] 5.689577e-05

title: Statistics
text:
Example 4-54: Tennis Tournament







Example 4-54 — Tennis Tournament


A tennis club has 8 married couples. If 1 man and 1 woman are selected at random to plan the tournament, find P(they are married to each other).






Total ways to select 1 man and 1 woman: 64 


Favorable outcomes (married couples): 8 


P(married couple) = 8 / 64 = 0.125 = 1/8



8 / (8 * 8)

[1] 0.125

text:
Chapter 4 Summary







Key Formulas









Concept
Formula




Classical probability
\(P(E) = n(E)/n(S)\)


Empirical probability
\(P(E) = f/n\)


Complement
\(P(\bar{E}) = 1 - P(E)\)


Addition Rule 1 (mutually exclusive)
\(P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B)\)


Addition Rule 2
\(P(A \text{ or } B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \text{ and } B)\)


Multiplication Rule 1 (independent)
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)\)


Multiplication Rule 2 (dependent)
\(P(A \text{ and } B) = P(A) \cdot P(B\|A)\)


Conditional probability
\(P(B\|A) = P(A \text{ and } B)/P(A)\)


Permutation
\(_nP_r = n!/(n-r)!\)


Combination
\(_nC_r = n!/[(n-r)!\,r!]\)
